task: Blue/green deploy with zero-downtime Socket.IO migration: drain blue, route new connections to green, hand off existing connections gracefully.
reasoning: Long-lived connections complicate blue/green. Strategy: announce migration on blue; clients reconnect to green via LB; blue drains. Combine sock-010 (graceful shutdown) with sock-306 (migrate signal).
why_this_is_correct: httpServer.close() stops accepting new connections (LB pulls blue from rotation soon). Migrate signal lets clients reconnect to green now. Grace period waits for self-disconnect; remaining sockets force-closed. Net effect: clients reconnect smoothly to green within a few seconds.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hard switch: clients disconnect en masse, all reconnect simultaneously to green = thundering herd. No announce: clients see TCP RST, reconnect to wherever the LB sends (could still be blue if DNS not updated).

task: Cap memory per room (e.g., limit chat history to 1000 messages). Evict oldest when full.
reasoning: Bounded queue per room. Push to tail, evict from head when over cap. Per-room separate structure. Memory-bounded.
why_this_is_correct: perRoomLimit caps memory per room. shift() is O(n) but n is bounded — acceptable. recent returns a copy so callers can't mutate internal state. totalSize for monitoring total messages.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Unbounded array per room: memory grows forever with active chat. Single global cap ignores per-room load balance. Returning the internal array lets callers mutate it accidentally.

task: Tolerate small clock skew in webhook signature verification by accepting a +/- 5min window.
reasoning: Webhook timestamps from providers may be slightly skewed. Pure-equality check fails when servers' clocks drift. Tolerance window lets legitimate webhooks through while still blocking replays.
why_this_is_correct: Window absorbs minor clock drift between provider and your server. 5min is the Stripe-style standard. Rejection still happens for genuinely stale or far-future requests. Timing-safe compare for the signature.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Strict equality on timestamp: legitimate webhooks rejected when clocks drift 30s. Huge tolerance (1h): replay window opens too wide.

task: Prototype pollution via JSON: attacker sends { '__proto__': { 'isAdmin': true } }. Block at the parser.
reasoning: JSON.parse doesn't pollute prototypes natively, but lodash merge / Object.assign on the parsed object can. Defense: parse with reviver that strips __proto__ keys, or use Object.create(null) as the base for merges.
why_this_is_correct: Reviver strips dangerous keys before they enter the object graph. safeMerge ignores them on merge. nullPrototypeBase converts to Object.create(null) which lacks the prototype chain entirely — defensive depth.
what_breaks_naive_approach: lodash _.merge with attacker-controlled source: { __proto__: { isAdmin: true } } pollutes Object.prototype, every object has isAdmin=true. Plain Object.assign doesn't pollute, but downstream usages might.

task: Effect calls async function but the API doesn't accept signal. Wrap with a 'mounted' flag pattern.
reasoning: Old APIs without AbortSignal can't be cancelled. Use a mounted/cancelled flag — discard the result if component unmounted before resolve.
why_this_is_correct: cancelled flag captured in cleanup. Resolve handlers check flag before setState. No setState on unmounted component. Works even when underlying API can't be cancelled.
what_breaks_naive_approach: setState after unmount: React warning + wasted work. Without the flag, late responses overwrite later-mounted instance state.

task: Stream endpoint leaks DB cursors when client disconnects. Detect disconnect, close cursor.
reasoning: Express req emits 'close' when client disconnects (network drop or browser navigated away). Long-running cursor must be closed in that handler to free DB resources.
why_this_is_correct: req.on('close') fires on client disconnect. cursor.close releases the DB resource. Aborted flag short-circuits the iteration loop. finally is the belt-and-suspenders — closes cursor if loop ended normally too.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Ignoring disconnect: cursor stays open consuming a connection. After many disconnections, you exhaust DB connection pool.

task: Feature-flagged middleware: only mount route when flag is enabled.
reasoning: Gate experimental endpoints behind a flag. Mount conditionally OR mount with a flag-checking guard that 404s when disabled.
why_this_is_correct: 404 on disabled feature = clients can't enumerate (vs 403 confirming existence). featureGate middleware is reusable across routes. Async flag lookup supports dynamic stores like Redis.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Conditional registration at boot: requires restart to flip flag. Returning 503: tells attackers the feature exists but is disabled.

task: Use explain('executionStats') to confirm an index is used before declaring a perf fix done.
reasoning: Adding an index is necessary but not sufficient. Verify Mongo actually uses it via explain. Look at winningPlan.inputStage.stage = IXSCAN, not COLLSCAN.
why_this_is_correct: executionStats reveals what the query optimizer actually did. COLLSCAN with high docsExamined = no index used. IXSCAN with low docsExamined/nReturned ratio close to 1 = good index. Always verify after index additions.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Assuming an index works because it exists: optimizer might pick the wrong one. Tuning without explain: guess-and-check on production data.

task: Split a tuple type into head and tail for typed variadic processing.
reasoning: TS infers tuple structure with template literals and conditional types. Useful for HOFs over args, middleware chains, etc.
why_this_is_correct: Variadic tuples with infer let TS peel off head/tail/last precisely. Runtime helpers preserve types via cast — the variadic guarantee is the type-level part. Foundation for typed currying, pipe, etc.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hand-typed overloads for fixed arities: doesn't scale. Returning unknown from head/tail forces consumer casts at every call.

task: Single middleware that logs every connection attempt with IP, transport, and outcome.
reasoning: One io.use that logs and passes through. Includes outcome via wrapping next.
why_this_is_correct: Attempt vs connected vs disconnected — three log lines per session, easy to count outcomes. Structured fields (transport, reason) drive dashboards.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Logging on connection only: missed handshakes invisible. Console.log unstructured: hard to query.

task: Logout endpoint that clears all auth cookies including session, refresh, and CSRF.
reasoning: Logout must clear EVERY auth-related cookie. Match name, path, domain — otherwise clear silently fails. Also invalidate server-side session.
why_this_is_correct: Server-side session destroyed first — token immediately invalid even if client keeps the cookie. Each cookie cleared with matching attributes (especially path). httpOnly mismatch on csrf reflects its actual setting.
what_breaks_naive_approach: clearCookie without path/domain: silently misses. Skipping server-side destroy: refresh cookie usable until expiry. Only clearing one cookie: others still authenticate.

task: Hydration mismatch from Date.now() in SSR. Defer to client effect.
reasoning: Server renders 'now: 10:00'; client renders 'now: 10:01' → mismatch error + re-render. Fix: render placeholder on server, populate after mount.
why_this_is_correct: Initial state is null → server renders '--:--:--'. Effect runs only on client → sets real time. suppressHydrationWarning on the span tells React to expect divergence here. Cleanup clears the interval.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useState(() => new Date().toLocaleTimeString()): server time and client time diverge, hydration warning. Different time text on server vs client = patched-but-broken DOM.

task: Configure perMessageDeflate for high-throughput servers — turn off for small frequent messages.
reasoning: WebSocket per-message-deflate compresses each frame. Beneficial for large/repetitive payloads; CPU overhead exceeds savings for small frames. Default is off in Socket.IO v4; reflect intent explicitly.
why_this_is_correct: Telemetry/chat apps with small payloads: compression hurts (CPU>bandwidth). Image/payload-heavy apps: compression helps. Explicit setting avoids relying on changing defaults across versions.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Enabling for tiny frames: CPU peg at high throughput. Disabling for large payloads: bandwidth and slow clients become bottlenecks.

task: Estimate password strength server-side using zxcvbn instead of arbitrary character-class rules.
reasoning: Length+character-class rules are weak (Password1! passes most). zxcvbn estimates entropy considering common patterns, dictionary words, sequences.
why_this_is_correct: zxcvbn score 3+ resists targeted attacks. Context words (email, name) prevent users using their own info. Suggestions returned for UI to show actionable feedback.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `^(?=.*[A-Z])(?=.*[0-9]).{12,}$`: passes Password12345. Doesn't catch keyboard walks (qwerty), dates, repeated patterns.

task: Accept a ref on a custom input wrapper so parents can call .focus() — React 19's plain ref prop.
reasoning: React 19 removes the forwardRef boilerplate. ref is just a prop. Pass through to the underlying DOM element.
why_this_is_correct: React 19's ref-as-prop removes the forwardRef wrapper. useImperativeHandle exposes a controlled API (focus + blur) rather than the raw DOM node — encapsulation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Passing inputRef directly via a custom prop name: caller writes special-case forwarding code. forwardRef wrapper: deprecated boilerplate in React 19.

task: GET handler runs business logic but HEAD requests waste resources. Short-circuit HEAD.
reasoning: Express auto-handles HEAD by running the GET handler and stripping body. Inefficient for expensive handlers. Implement explicit HEAD that returns just headers.
why_this_is_correct: HEAD checks existence cheaply (Mongo exists() returns minimal data). GET does the full fetch. Clients using HEAD for cache validation don't pull the whole doc just to learn it exists.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Letting Express run GET for HEAD: expensive query for nothing. Returning 200 unconditionally on HEAD: clients think a deleted resource still exists.

task: Evolve a service interface without breaking existing callers — add optional methods, deprecate gradually.
reasoning: Adding required methods to an interface breaks all impls. Add as optional (with default impl in base class), then over time make required.
why_this_is_correct: V2 extends V1 — old impls still work as V1. Optional methods at first (findManyByIds?). Adapter provides default impl using V1 primitives so callers can rely on V2 even with V1-only impls.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding required methods to V1: all existing impls break at compile. Versioned classes without an adapter: callers must care which version they have.

task: For high-write time-series data (metrics, logs), use a capped collection to auto-prune old entries.
reasoning: Capped collections have fixed size; oldest documents auto-removed when full. Insertion order preserved, sequential disk access — very fast writes. Trade-off: no deletes, limited updates.
why_this_is_correct: 100MB or 1M docs cap. New writes silently evict oldest. w:0 write concern fire-and-forget for max throughput on telemetry. Time-based index for queries.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Uncapped time-series: collection grows forever. TTL index (auto-delete by expireAfterSeconds) is the more flexible alternative for date-based deletion — both have their place; capped is best for fixed-size sliding windows.

task: Hybrid pattern: do one prerequisite operation, then fan out N parallel operations.
reasoning: Common: load user, then load all of user's child resources in parallel. await the prereq, Promise.all the rest.
why_this_is_correct: Prereq (user) sequential — we need user.orderIds first. Children parallel for max throughput. Promise.all fail-fast on any order load failure; switch to allSettled if partial failure is acceptable.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sequential fetchOrder loop: N round-trips serialized, latency = sum. Promise.all over [fetchUser, fetchOrders]: can't fan out orders before knowing the ids.

task: Narrow `unknown` to specific types with typeof, instanceof, and Array.isArray.
reasoning: unknown forces narrowing. typeof for primitives. instanceof for class instances. Array.isArray for arrays. Object check via typeof === 'object' && value !== null.
why_this_is_correct: Every branch narrows value's type so TS allows accessing type-specific properties (.length on arrays, .message on Error). Number.isFinite catches NaN. Functions checked by typeof.
what_breaks_naive_approach: typeof value === 'object' returns true for null and arrays — explicit checks needed. instanceof across realms (iframes) fails — use structural checks if cross-realm matters.

task: Minimal but production-safe Socket.IO server: typed events, auth, CORS, rate limit on connection.
reasoning: Starter template that bundles common production defaults. Less likely to ship a misconfigured server.
why_this_is_correct: Typed event maps. Auth via JWT. CORS scoped. Payload limit. WebSocket-only avoids sticky-session complexity. Sensible ping intervals. Algorithm pinning on JWT.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default config: open CORS, large buffer, no auth, polling+websocket needs sticky. Each is a foot-gun.

task: Cap input lengths on all string fields at parse time — defends against ReDoS and DoS via huge inputs.
reasoning: Zod min/max for every string. Centralize sensible defaults via a helper. Each field still tunable.
why_this_is_correct: Every string field has an explicit cap. Helpers (shortText, mediumText) standardize sizes. Email/URL fields use sensible RFC limits. Slug regex bounds to safe range.
what_breaks_naive_approach: z.string() without max: attackers send 100MB strings, eat memory + regex CPU. Each field hand-typing maxes inconsistently. Email without max accepts megabytes.

task: Hook that schedules a callback to fire on an interval, handling cleanup and changing delay.
reasoning: Like Dan Abramov's useInterval. Stores callback in a ref so changing it doesn't reset the timer; effect resets only on delay change.
why_this_is_correct: Callback stored in ref — changing it doesn't restart the timer. Interval changes only on delayMs change. null delay pauses. Cleanup clears the interval on unmount or delay change.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Including callback in deps: timer restarts every render. Calling callback directly: stale closure issues. Skipping cleanup: interval keeps firing after unmount.

task: Catch unhandledRejection at process level and log structured detail — second-to-last defense.
reasoning: Even with careful code, a missed .catch can cause unhandled rejection. Global handler logs and decides (crash vs continue). Combine with err-107.
why_this_is_correct: Logged with structure (level, name, message, stack). Setting exitCode = process exits 1 after current event loop drains — orchestrator restarts. Some teams prefer log-and-continue; pick based on app criticality.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default Node behavior since 15.x: unhandledRejection terminates the process. No logging = you don't know why. Suppressing the event without logging hides bugs.

task: Use UUID v7 (time-ordered) for entity IDs to get both randomness and DB-friendly insert ordering.
reasoning: UUID v4 is random — B-tree inserts scatter, hurting write throughput at scale. UUID v7 prepends ms timestamp; lexicographic order matches insertion time; locality of reference on writes.
why_this_is_correct: First 48 bits = milliseconds since epoch. Version (4 bits) = 7. Variant bits set per RFC. Remaining 62 bits = random. Lexically sortable by creation time → B-tree inserts hit recent pages, better cache locality, fewer page splits.
what_breaks_naive_approach: UUID v4 IDs scatter across B-tree: every insert hits random pages, cache miss, page splits, slow writes at scale. Sequential auto-increment IDs leak count, enable enumeration.

task: Use slim projection to shrink list responses — return only fields the UI needs.
reasoning: List endpoints often return entire entities when UI shows 3 fields. Projection cuts payload size, JSON parse cost on client, and DB I/O.
why_this_is_correct: select() limits returned fields. Payload size proportional to fields, not entity size. Mapper converts to API shape. lean for plain objects.
what_breaks_naive_approach: find() with no projection: returns all fields including big ones (bio, jsonblobs). 100x the bandwidth for the same UI.

task: Forward client-side errors over Socket.IO to backend for centralized logging.
reasoning: Frontend errors normally go to Sentry. For internal apps without Sentry, push errors via socket. Server logs them with the user/session context.
why_this_is_correct: Zod validates the incoming shape so malformed reports can't crash the handler. Length caps prevent abuse. Server-side log enriched with userId from socket.data. Single source of structured error logs.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Logging raw client payloads: attackers send huge strings to fill log storage. No validation: parser exceptions in log pipeline.

task: Header X-User-Id from frontend is trusted — attacker spoofs it to impersonate. Strip such headers at the boundary.
reasoning: Headers should never carry identity unless the gateway sets them. If your LB sets X-User-Id after validation, OK. Otherwise reject or ignore.
why_this_is_correct: stripReservedHeaders removes attacker-controlled identity headers. gatewayInjectedAuth trusts only headers set by the LB (network-isolated, not reachable from client). Pair with allowlisting incoming traffic to LB only.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `req.userId = req.header('X-User-Id')`: any client posts header, gets that identity. Total auth bypass.

task: Conditional render: return null vs fragment vs an actual placeholder — when each is right.
reasoning: Return null to render nothing. Fragment for grouping multi-element output. Placeholder for layout preservation. Each has subtle effects on layout and tests.
why_this_is_correct: unmount: zero render cost, full state reset when remounted. hide: keeps state, just visually hidden — good for tabs that should retain scroll/input. placeholder: reserves layout space.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Always returning null: tabs lose state when switching back. Always hiding via CSS: expensive trees stay mounted forever. Mismatched aria-hidden = screen reader still announces hidden content.

task: Differentiate /healthz (liveness) from /readyz (readiness). Liveness lightweight; readiness checks dependencies.
reasoning: k8s probes have distinct intents. Liveness: am I alive? Readiness: am I ready to serve? Both endpoints; readiness can fail without restart.
why_this_is_correct: healthz always 200 if the process is running — k8s only restarts if the process is wedged. readyz returns 503 when deps are down — LB pulls pod from rotation but doesn't restart. Distinct intents = correct behavior.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Same endpoint for both: transient Redis hiccup restarts the pod, mass restart loop. Heavy DB queries in liveness: probe times out under load, pod cycle.

task: Feature-folder barrel that exports ONLY the public surface; tests can still reach internals via relative imports.
reasoning: Barrel curates public API. Tests use relative imports for internals (intentional). Lint rule prevents non-test consumers from doing the same.
why_this_is_correct: Convention: _internal prefix marks private. Lint blocks external imports of _internal. Tests in feature folder use relative imports freely. Public API surface = barrel exports only.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Exporting everything from barrel: consumers couple to internal types. No naming convention for internals: hard to enforce later. Lint rule without exceptions for tests: blocks legitimate test access.

task: Differences between never, unknown, void — and when each is the right return type.
reasoning: never: function never returns (throws or infinite loop). unknown: value of unknown type, must narrow. void: function returns nothing meaningful (caller shouldn't use the return).
why_this_is_correct: never is bottom — assignable to anything, nothing assignable to it (except never). unknown is top — only assignable to itself/any, requires narrowing. void is special: function-type position lets ANY return (it's discarded), but value-type position is undefined.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Returning any from JSON.parse: callers don't narrow, runtime crashes. void confused with undefined: void parameter accepts undefined but undefined doesn't accept void in function position.

task: Synchronize per-room state across cluster nodes via Redis pub/sub — broadcasts are NOT enough.
reasoning: Redis adapter forwards emits between nodes. But shared in-memory state (sock-001's RoomRegistry) doesn't sync. Either store state in Redis directly or use pub/sub to propagate state changes.
why_this_is_correct: Pub/sub propagates changes to all nodes; each node applies locally. instanceId check prevents own messages from being re-applied. Eventually consistent — short delay between change and propagation, acceptable for membership.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-instance Map without sync: members() returns only this node's view. Querying Socket.IO adapter for membership doesn't preserve custom metadata.

task: Set Permissions-Policy to disable browser features you don't need (camera, microphone, geolocation).
reasoning: Permissions-Policy header restricts browser APIs. Disable what you don't use. Defense against XSS that tries to grab camera/mic.
why_this_is_correct: Empty () denies all origins. (self) allows only your origin. Restrictive default = XSS can't escalate to camera/mic capture. Helmet has limited support; setting manually gives full control.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Not setting: browser defaults often allow APIs that XSS can abuse. Setting (*) for fullscreen-style features: any iframe can use them too.

task: Duplicate keys in lists trigger React warnings and break reconciliation. Detect and de-dupe deterministically.
reasoning: Keys must be unique within siblings. With possibly-duplicate ids (legacy data), suffix by index. Or de-dup on the server side and surface a warning.
why_this_is_correct: Helper suffixes duplicate ids deterministically; first occurrence keeps its id, subsequent ones get __1, __2. _renderKey added to the item so it's available alongside the original id.
what_breaks_naive_approach: key={item.id} with duplicates: React reuses the wrong DOM nodes — state/input swap unpredictably. key={`${item.id}-${index}`}: defeats reconciliation entirely on reorder.

task: Properly fire-and-forget a promise so it doesn't trigger unhandled rejection if it fails.
reasoning: void operator marks intent to discard. Add .catch to log; otherwise unhandled rejection kills process (Node 15+).
why_this_is_correct: void signals 'I'm intentionally discarding'. .catch captures rejection so it doesn't become unhandled. label gives the log enough context to find the source.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `metrics.recordEvent(...);` without catch or await: rejection goes unhandled, process exits in Node 15+. `metrics.recordEvent(...).catch(() => {})`: swallow errors silently, lose all observability.

task: Generic key-getter typed to ensure the key actually exists on the object type.
reasoning: Extract<keyof T, …> for restricting allowed keys. Or `K extends keyof T` directly.
why_this_is_correct: K extends keyof T = compile-time check that key exists. T[K] indexes the value type precisely. Misspelled keys fail at compile.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `pluck<T>(items, key: string)`: any string accepted, return type unknown. Cast `items.map(i => i[key])` widely accepted but loses types.

task: Detect rooms exceeding a member threshold and switch to a low-overhead emit strategy.
reasoning: Very large rooms (1000s of sockets) benefit from compression off and volatile emit for non-critical updates. Auto-detect via room size; switch tactics.
why_this_is_correct: Small rooms: simple broadcast. Large rooms: compression off (CPU dominates), per-socket emit to allow per-socket flags. Best-effort + large = volatile (drops on slow clients) for high-frequency updates.
what_breaks_naive_approach: One emit strategy for all rooms: small rooms pay no penalty but large rooms suffer compression CPU. No volatile option for non-critical updates: buffers grow for slow clients.

task: Cache CORS preflight via Access-Control-Max-Age so browsers don't re-OPTIONS every request.
reasoning: Each non-simple request triggers an OPTIONS preflight unless cached. Max-Age (in seconds) tells the browser how long to cache. Reduces server load + latency.
why_this_is_correct: maxAge: 86400 = 24h preflight cache. Chrome caps at 2h regardless; Firefox honors longer. Still significant savings — preflights drop dramatically.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No maxAge: OPTIONS request before every non-simple request, doubling round trips. Setting maxAge:0 explicitly: disables cache.

task: Hook that returns the previous value of a prop or state, useful for detecting changes.
reasoning: Common pattern: react to value transitions. usePrevious holds the previous render's value in a ref, updated after each commit.
why_this_is_correct: Ref updated in useEffect = after commit, so the next render sees the previous value. Returning ref.current gives the value from the prior render. queueMicrotask defers the setState to avoid setting state during render.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Updating ref in render: returns current value on next render — but read happens before update, so logic gets stale. Using state for previous: triggers extra render on every change.

task: Walk an Error's cause chain when reporting so the full causal path is captured in logs.
reasoning: Error.cause (Node 16.9+) lets you wrap errors with context. Reporting tools (Sentry) auto-walks the chain; for custom logging, walk it yourself.
why_this_is_correct: Depth cap prevents pathological infinite chains. Each level captured separately for clean logs. cause chain reveals the actual root cause buried under wrappers.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Logging err.message alone: wrapper messages mask root cause. Logging full err object: serializes oddly, hard to read.

task: Logger as an injected interface so production uses structured JSON, tests can assert log calls.
reasoning: Hardcoded console.log makes test assertions impossible. Inject a Logger interface; provide a stdoutJsonLogger for prod and an inMemoryLogger for tests.
why_this_is_correct: Interface-based logging = test-friendly. JsonLogger emits ND-JSON ready for log aggregators. InMemoryLogger collects entries for test assertions: `expect(logger.entries).toContainEqual(...)`.
what_breaks_naive_approach: console.log everywhere: format inconsistent, no levels, hard to redirect. Tests can't assert log behavior without monkey-patching console.

task: Tune cursor batchSize to balance memory vs round trips when iterating large collections.
reasoning: Default batchSize is 1000 (or driver-dependent). Smaller = less memory per batch but more round trips. Larger = fewer trips but more memory at once.
why_this_is_correct: batchSize controls server prefetch. Code batch (separate) controls business processing chunk. Smaller batches = less peak memory; larger = better throughput. 500 is a reasonable starting point.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default batchSize 1000 with heavy docs: ~MB per batch in memory. Processing one-at-a-time without code batching: O(N) round trips when bulk operations are available.

task: Optimistic lock with explicit version field instead of __v, for consumers that filter __v out.
reasoning: Some apps strip __v in API responses. Use a custom 'revision' field for the same semantics — explicit, visible.
why_this_is_correct: Visible revision field in API responses so clients can use it for ETag-style updates. Same semantics as __v but explicit. Conflict response includes current state for client-side reconciliation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hiding __v from API but using it server-side: clients can't optimistic-update because they don't see the version. Stripping in toJSON without an alternative: lose conflict detection.

task: Generic factory class that creates strongly-typed entities with shared utilities.
reasoning: Single generic factory eliminates boilerplate per entity. Constraints on T ensure common shape (id, timestamps).
why_this_is_correct: Generic class enforces shape constraints once. create auto-fills id and timestamps; touch updates updatedAt automatically. Reusable across all entity types.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-entity factory: copy-paste of id/timestamp logic. Forgetting to set updatedAt on touch: stale timestamps mask edits.

task: Show a user-visible 'reconnecting...' toast when the socket loses connection.
reasoning: Network drops are common on mobile. UX: don't pretend to be connected. Sub to connection events; toast as state changes.
why_this_is_correct: Distinguish 'reconnecting' (transient) from 'offline' (gave up). Reconnect events live on socket.io (Manager), not socket directly. Toast hidden when connected — no visual noise on healthy connections.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No status indicator: user sends messages into the void. Always-on indicator: distracting when connected.

task: Reject TRACE explicitly even if methodAllowlist isn't in use.
reasoning: TRACE method can echo headers (incl. cookies) — Cross-Site Tracing risk. Even with allowlist, defense-in-depth: explicitly 405 TRACE.
why_this_is_correct: TRACK is IIS's variant — block too. 405 with Allow header per RFC. Allowlist (sec-316) is stronger, but explicit deny is good defense-in-depth.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Allowing TRACE: under right conditions (httponly bypass), can exfiltrate cookies.

task: Async initial state via useEffect + useState (since useState's initializer must be sync).
reasoning: useState's initializer can't be async. Pattern: useState(null) for the initial; useEffect to fetch and set. Better: Suspense (state-103) for newer patterns. Both have a place.
why_this_is_correct: null + loading flag covers the 'no data yet' window. cancelled guard prevents setting state after unmount or id change. Pattern works without Suspense for older React.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useState(async () => fetch(...)): initializer must be sync, returns a Promise as the state value — useless. Skipping cancelled flag: stale data from old id overwrites new id's data.

task: Returning 501 Not Implemented for placeholder endpoints during scaffolding.
reasoning: During development, defining route shells. 501 communicates 'this exists but isn't done' — useful for client devs working on parallel.
why_this_is_correct: 501 = correct semantics: server understands the request but won't fulfill it. Frontend can detect 501 and mock the response while backend builds. Cleaner than 404 (which means 'unknown route').
what_breaks_naive_approach: Stubbing with TODO and returning 200 with fake data: frontend builds against fake data; integration fails on real impl. 404 misleads — looks like missing route.

task: Centralize magic numbers and string constants so changes happen in one place.
reasoning: Sprinkled `30 * 60 * 1000` and `'admin'` across files = drift. Define named constants with intent-revealing names.
why_this_is_correct: Named constants explain intent. Time units composed from base values. Roles as const + derived type prevents typos. Limits centralized for tuning.
what_breaks_naive_approach: 30 * 60 * 1000 scattered: changing TTL means grep + manual edit. Magic strings 'admin' invite typos no compiler catches.

task: Skip compression for SSE/streaming endpoints — compression buffers defeat streaming.
reasoning: compression middleware buffers until threshold. For SSE, that buffers events. Skip via filter function (already in perf-107) but worth highlighting.
why_this_is_correct: Three layers of detection: response Content-Type, multipart, request Accept header. SSE/streaming bypass compression entirely so events flush per write. Multipart also bypassed because it's typically large pre-compressed content.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default compression on SSE: 'no events received' until enough bytes accumulate for compression. Confusing debugging session ensues.

task: Use readonly arrays in function signatures to communicate 'I don't mutate this'.
reasoning: `ReadonlyArray<T>` or `readonly T[]` prevents the function from mutating. Callers can pass mutable arrays freely (covariance). Defensive choice for utility functions.
why_this_is_correct: readonly in signature is a contract — function won't push/pop/sort the input. Callers can pass mutable arrays. Mutable input parameters are a footgun; default to readonly unless you really need to mutate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `items: T[]` then `items.sort()` mutates caller's array — surprising bug. readonly defends against accidental mutation.

task: Send ArrayBuffer payloads efficiently — server reads bytes without JSON overhead.
reasoning: Native binary transport in Socket.IO. Client sends ArrayBuffer; server receives Buffer. No JSON encoding/decoding.
why_this_is_correct: Direct binary path avoids base64 inflation. Size limit (1MB) prevents abuse. Type guard handles either ArrayBuffer or Buffer (depending on client). Pair with sock-015 for global cap.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Base64-encoding payloads before send: 33% bandwidth waste + parse cost. Accepting unlimited size: memory exhaustion on malicious clients.

task: Configurable payload-size middleware that returns structured 413 for too-large requests.
reasoning: Different endpoints have different size profiles (image upload vs JSON form). Per-route limit + structured response. Avoids one-size-fits-all global cap.
why_this_is_correct: Per-route limit matches endpoint expectations. Structured 413 with limit info lets clients show 'try a shorter input'. Catching entity.too.large at the per-parser layer keeps the error handler clean.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Global 100kb limit: comments accept too-much, articles get cut off. Default 413 HTML page: JSON clients explode.

task: Effect that should run only once on mount — useEffect with [] vs handling deps correctly.
reasoning: useEffect(fn, []) runs once but ignores any captured props/state. If you need values from props, include them in deps or use a ref to read current values without retriggering.
why_this_is_correct: StrictMode should run setup, cleanup, then setup again in development. The hook does not suppress the second setup, so subscriptions remain active after the development remount while cleanup remains symmetric.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A calledRef guard can run setup once, then StrictMode cleanup removes the subscription and the second setup is skipped. The component stays mounted but unsubscribed, which hides the real lifecycle bug.

task: Add a catch-all route as the last middleware that returns 404 JSON for unknown paths.
reasoning: Express default 404 is HTML. JSON-consuming clients break. Last-middleware catch-all returns JSON.
why_this_is_correct: Must be the LAST middleware (after all routes). Throws NotFoundError → flows through error middleware → JSON 404. Single catch-all instead of per-method 404 handlers.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default Express 404: returns 'Cannot GET /foo' HTML. Per-router catch-all only catches within one router, not across the app.

task: Provider interface lets you swap real Stripe with a mock for tests without changing any caller.
reasoning: Test environment shouldn't hit real Stripe. Interface in domain; real impl in production wiring; in-memory impl for tests.
why_this_is_correct: Same interface; production injects Stripe; tests inject InMemory. InMemory has a charges array for assertions. Special tokens (declined) simulate failure paths without network.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Direct Stripe SDK calls in services: tests hit real Stripe (paid) or get rate-limited. Mocking Stripe SDK directly: brittle, breaks on SDK upgrades.

task: Stable key from item id; avoid array index for re-orderable lists (revisited from perf-007).
reasoning: Repeated to emphasize foundational nature. Different angle: use this knowledge to debug reorder bugs in code review.
why_this_is_correct: Stable id-based key preserves component identity across reorder. Internal state (checkbox) stays with the right todo.
what_breaks_naive_approach: key={index}: reorder shuffles state to wrong todos. key={todo.text}: editing text changes key, component remounts losing focus.

task: Emit to multiple targeted rooms in one call with io.to([roomA, roomB]).
reasoning: io.to accepts string or string[]. Single call hits all listed rooms. With adapter, one fan-out instead of N round trips.
why_this_is_correct: Array of rooms = single fan-out. Adapter handles cluster distribution. Empty check avoids no-op call.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Loop of io.to(room).emit per room: N adapter calls. With Redis adapter, N pub/sub messages instead of 1.

task: Reject requests without proper Content-Type on JSON endpoints to prevent CSRF via simple POST.
reasoning: Simple POSTs (text/plain, form data) skip CORS preflight. Requiring application/json forces preflight, blocking CSRF. Validate at parser.
why_this_is_correct: The guard runs before body semantics and keys off HTTP method, so simple text/plain or form POSTs cannot slip through just because req.body is undefined. Requiring application/json forces a CORS preflight for cross-origin JSON mutations.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Accepting any Content-Type: simple-POST CSRF works (form-encoded body that maps to your JSON API).

task: Stabilize an array prop with useMemo so memoized children don't re-render when the parent does.
reasoning: Arrays created inline are new references every render. Memoize when feeding into React.memo or useEffect deps.
why_this_is_correct: filtered is memoized — when unrelated changes, filtered reference is stable, List skips re-render. Without useMemo, new array each render = List re-renders every time.
what_breaks_naive_approach: <List items={allItems.filter(...)} />: new array each render. Memoizing the entire component but passing fresh array each render = memo's perf benefit lost.

task: Express error middleware MUST have 4 parameters — show why and provide a typed wrapper.
reasoning: Express identifies error middleware by Function.length === 4. If you write 3 params, it's treated as regular middleware. Wrapper enforces.
why_this_is_correct: Wrapper preserves 4-arg signature. TS catches missing args. Function.length stays 4 because arrow signature has 4 named params.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `(req, res, next) => {}` for an error handler: Express never invokes it as error middleware. Bugs vanish silently.

task: Always-valid value object (parsed at construction, immutable after).
reasoning: Parse-don't-validate pattern: convert raw input to a typed VO at the boundary. Code that handles VOs never re-checks.
why_this_is_correct: Constructors private; only static factory validates. Once you have a PositiveInteger, you never have to check its sign. applyDiscount signature documents constraints in types.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain number params: every function re-checks (or doesn't). Validation logic duplicated across the codebase.

task: Reduce over a large list to compute a single value — memoize so it doesn't re-run on every render.
reasoning: reduce is O(n). Re-running per render is wasted work. useMemo with [list] dep recomputes only when list changes.
why_this_is_correct: Single useMemo computes all three aggregates in one pass. Recomputed only when orders reference changes. Caller is responsible for keeping orders referentially stable.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Computing inline (`orders.reduce(...)` in JSX): three full passes per render. Storing aggregates in state with useEffect: duplicate state, sync issues.

task: Process items with controlled concurrency AND order preservation.
reasoning: mapWithConcurrency (conc-004) gives concurrency but uses index-based assignment. This explicit version shows the order guarantee.
why_this_is_correct: results[i] indexed by input position — completion order doesn't matter. Persistent workers pull next index = natural concurrency cap.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Promise.all(items.map(fn)) — no concurrency cap, exhausts resources. Pushing to results[] as items complete: scrambled order.

task: Narrow object types using the `in` operator instead of property access.
reasoning: `'prop' in value` narrows to types that have prop. Useful when union members lack a clear discriminator.
why_this_is_correct: `in` narrows the union to members containing that key. No discriminator field needed. Type-safe access to the narrowed branch's specific properties.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Accessing contact.phone without narrowing: error or `unknown`. Adding a discriminator field everywhere adds noise when the structural difference is enough.

task: Async io.use middleware that connects to a DB on connect — never block the event loop on slow ops.
reasoning: io.use accepts async. But slow checks delay handshake. Use a cache for lookups; pre-flight expensive checks outside the middleware.
why_this_is_correct: Async middleware lets you check the DB. Slow-loader warning surfaces when middleware becomes a latency bottleneck. Cached loader (e.g., sec-305 UserContextCache) keeps it fast.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Long DB query in middleware: handshake latency, retries pile up. Sync code paths in async middleware: missed await means race conditions.

task: Admin endpoints behind a separate auth path (not JWT) — single admin token, env-only, no user database.
reasoning: For internal admin actions, sharing user auth is overkill. Static admin token stored in env, compared timing-safely. Limit to specific IPs + audit log.
why_this_is_correct: Single static token from env. timing-safe compare prevents byte-leak. Audit log records every admin access. Pair with sec-104 IP allowlist for defense-in-depth.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `token === expectedToken`: timing leak. Sharing user JWT for admin: requires admin user accounts + role checks scattered. No audit log = no trace of admin actions.

task: Reducer with strongly typed action creators for typo-safe dispatch.
reasoning: Action creators encapsulate the action shape; dispatchers call them by name. IDE autocomplete + compile-time check on payload.
why_this_is_correct: Centralized creators = single source of truth for action shapes. Renaming a type updates everywhere. Hard to misspell action types because you go through the creator.
what_breaks_naive_approach: dispatch({ type: 'INK' }) typo: TS catches if action type is union literal. Without creators, payload shape drifts across call sites.

task: Strip stack traces from production error responses while keeping them in logs.
reasoning: Stacks reveal file paths, third-party versions, etc. Log full stack; respond with sanitized message.
why_this_is_correct: Logs always have the stack for debugging. Production responses hide it. Dev keeps it for ergonomics. Boolean isProd makes the toggle explicit at composition.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Returning err.stack in production: leaks file paths, library versions. No log: stack invisible everywhere.

task: Named exports + factory function instead of default export — better for refactoring and IDE rename.
reasoning: Default exports lose name when imported (renamed freely). Refactoring is harder. Named exports keep identity stable, enable per-symbol auto-imports.
why_this_is_correct: Named exports = stable symbol names across the codebase. IDE rename refactors all usages. Auto-import works per symbol. Tree-shaking is unaffected.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default exports renamed arbitrarily by each consumer: grep doesn't find usages. Renaming the source means manually updating every import.

task: Bulk update many docs with one updateMany instead of N findByIdAndUpdate.
reasoning: updateMany sends one query covering all matches. Iterating per-doc = N round trips. Use updateMany for uniform updates.
why_this_is_correct: Single query updates all matching docs server-side. modifiedCount reports actual changes. Atomic per-doc (Mongo guarantees). Index on (status, lastLoginAt) makes it fast.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Iterating: find().then(docs => Promise.all(docs.map(update))): N round trips, slow at scale, no atomicity vs concurrent edits.

task: Why AbortController is preferred over Promise.race for cancellation.
reasoning: Promise.race times out the wait but the underlying work continues. AbortController cancels the actual operation. Use AbortController when supported.
why_this_is_correct: AbortController propagates cancellation down to the network stack. Promise.race only wins the wait — the loser keeps running until naturally completes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Promise.race timeout fires; caller moves on; in the background fetch finishes 30s later, still parsing response, holding sockets. At scale = leak.

task: Pass an array to a function with readonly param to defend against accidental mutation.
reasoning: Default `T[]` param allows the function to mutate caller's array. ReadonlyArray<T> param signals intent and blocks mutation.
why_this_is_correct: ReadonlyArray param: TS rejects .sort/.push/etc. on items. Copy with spread first, then mutate the copy. Caller's array unchanged.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `items: T[]` and `items.sort(...)`: mutates caller's array, sort order shows up unexpectedly elsewhere. Hard-to-trace bug.

task: Use io.of() to scope events to a feature namespace — avoid one giant default namespace.
reasoning: Namespaces give a clean separation. /chat for chat events; /notifications for notifications. Per-namespace middleware. Reduces accidental cross-feature emits.
why_this_is_correct: Each namespace has its own event registry; emits in /chat don't leak to /notifications. Per-namespace middleware enforces feature-specific auth. Clients connect to specific namespaces (io('/chat')).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single namespace with everything: noisy emits, accidental name collisions, single global middleware that must cover all cases.

task: Strip incoming X-Forwarded-Host (and rely on trust proxy + Host header) to prevent host header injection.
reasoning: X-Forwarded-Host can be set by attacker. trust proxy only validates X-Forwarded-For/Proto. Strip X-Forwarded-Host explicitly.
why_this_is_correct: Removes spoofable host-related headers before downstream code reads them. Pair with hostHeaderGuard (sec-306) to validate Host itself. Defense-in-depth against host-header injection (cache poisoning, password reset link forgery).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting X-Forwarded-Host for absolute URL building: attacker sends X-Forwarded-Host: attacker.com → password reset emails point to attacker.

task: Action with optional payload — type the union to make payload required only when relevant.
reasoning: Some actions have payload, some don't. Discriminated union per action ensures payload only typed when needed.
why_this_is_correct: Each variant declares its required payload fields. dispatch({ type: 'CLEAR' }) compiles without payload; dispatch({ type: 'ADD' }) doesn't (missing item).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single Action { type; item?; query? }: every case body checks for the field, easy to forget; payload type ambiguous.

task: Abstract AppError base class so subclasses must declare statusCode and code.
reasoning: Abstract members force subclasses to be complete. Constructor centralizes name + stack setup. Less per-subclass boilerplate.
why_this_is_correct: abstract enforces declarations in subclasses — can't create an AppError without status/code. Base sets name from constructor.name so logs show the right class. captureStackTrace removes the constructor from the stack.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Manual super() + setting fields in each subclass: copy-paste, easy to forget captureStackTrace, name set wrong.

task: Fluent builder where each step returns this — typed chain that supports method chaining.
reasoning: Returning this from setters enables chaining: builder.setX().setY().build(). Generic this type preserves precise subclass when used through inheritance.
why_this_is_correct: return this on every setter. Chain reads like a sentence. build() materializes immutable result. Validation inside setters throws early.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Returning new instances per setter: more allocations, less ergonomic. Returning specific class type instead of this: breaks subclass chaining.

task: Render multiple top-level elements per item using Fragment with key.
reasoning: Two-element rows (term + definition) need both keyed together. Fragment with key serves.
why_this_is_correct: Fragment groups dt+dd as a logical row. Key on Fragment is honored (unlike <> shorthand). No extra DOM wrapper that would break dl semantics.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping in <div> breaks dl semantics. <> </> can't carry a key. Returning arrays from .map without keyed Fragments = warning.

task: Run N async tasks in parallel, each with its own timeout; collect results including timeouts.
reasoning: Combines per-call timeout with allSettled-style collection. Each result is success or specific failure (timeout vs error).
why_this_is_correct: Each task races against its own timeout. allSettled-style results so one timeout doesn't fail the batch. Discriminated reason field lets caller act differently per outcome.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Promise.all on timed tasks: first failure rejects the batch. Single shared timeout: slow task eats budget for fast ones.

task: Readonly Record from const tuple — derive both the key union and the value type.
reasoning: Constants stored as objects benefit from `as const` for value preservation + Record type for shape.
why_this_is_correct: as const preserves literal values (true vs boolean). satisfies enforces shape. FlagName derived = type-safe access. Refactoring flag names propagates.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hand-typed `type FlagName = 'newUi' | ...`: drifts. Type annotation `: Record<string, boolean>` widens values, losing const precision.

task: Force each user into a stable per-user room on connection so server can target them by userId.
reasoning: `user:<id>` room joined on connect. io.to(`user:<id>`).emit lets server reach any user's sockets, including multi-tab.
why_this_is_correct: Every connection of the same user lands in the same room. emitToUser fans out to all their sockets via adapter (multi-instance). Disconnect on no-userId prevents anonymous connections.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tracking socket ids per user manually: doesn't span cluster, and breaks on multi-tab.

task: Avoid prototype pollution in custom JSON.stringify replacer functions.
reasoning: Custom replacers might inadvertently expose __proto__ in output. Use replacer to filter sensitive fields too.
why_this_is_correct: Replacer filters dangerous keys and redacts sensitive ones at serialization. Used for log lines and error responses. Lowercase comparison catches case variations.
what_breaks_naive_approach: JSON.stringify without replacer: passwords logged in plain text. Including __proto__ in custom serialization: prototype pollution downstream if re-parsed.

task: Choose ref vs state: ref for values that don't trigger render; state for UI-driving values.
reasoning: Mutating ref.current doesn't re-render. Use for click counts you log, scroll positions you read, latest values. Use state for anything the UI displays.
why_this_is_correct: totalScrolled in ref because UI doesn't show it directly — no need to re-render on every scroll tick. showHint in state because it changes the rendered output. Only one setState fires when threshold crossed.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useState(0) for scroll total: re-render per scroll event = jank. Ref for showHint: UI doesn't update when threshold is crossed.

task: Translate Zod issues into user-friendly messages per field with a small map.
reasoning: Default Zod messages are technical. Map common error codes to friendly text. Per-field override for context-aware wording.
why_this_is_correct: Per-field overrides for context (password length, email format). Defaults for codes without specific override. flattenFriendly produces a per-field map ready for forms.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Showing Zod's raw 'String must contain at least 12 character(s)' to users: technically true but cold. Hand-rolling messages per Zod schema: drift.

task: Annotate interface stability with naming conventions (Public, Internal) so deprecation is visible.
reasoning: Mark public API stability via class/interface name (UserServicePublic vs UserServiceInternal). Documents intent and helps ESLint enforce.
why_this_is_correct: Naming makes stability visible at type level. ESLint rule could block UserServiceInternal imports from HTTP layer (only internal jobs allowed). Documents which methods are 'safe to depend on'.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single interface mixing public and internal methods: consumers can't tell what's stable. Breaking 'internal' methods causes ripples to surprised public consumers.

task: Run an effect only when a condition is true (e.g., user is logged in) without using nested ifs everywhere.
reasoning: useEffect runs on every render with new deps. Inside, early-return if condition fails. Cleaner than wrapping every effect access.
why_this_is_correct: Early return inside the effect skips the work AND skips registering cleanup when condition is false. Re-runs when isAuthenticated flips. Cleanup runs when condition flips false (because effect re-runs).
what_breaks_naive_approach: if (isAuth) useEffect(...): conditional hooks violate React rules. Putting condition in fn body but registering setInterval anyway: wasted resources when unauthenticated.

task: for-of + await runs sequentially. for...of with forEach + async is a common bug.
reasoning: forEach doesn't await async callbacks — they all start at once. for-of with await is what you want for sequential.
why_this_is_correct: for-of yields the event loop between awaits, processing one at a time. forEach doesn't accept async meaningfully — it discards the promise. The bug is invisible in trivial demos but breaks under any async work.
what_breaks_naive_approach: forEach + async: 1000 parallel fn calls thunder upstream service. Looks sequential in code, isn't.

task: When to use a TypeScript `as` cast and when it is a bug: show safe DOM/library-narrowing uses, replace unsafe API/JSON casts with runtime validation, and explain the tradeoffs.
reasoning: `as` bypasses TS — sometimes necessary (DOM narrowing, library quirks), often a sign of missing type info. Use narrowly with comments.
why_this_is_correct: DOM narrowing: getElementById returns HTMLElement | null, but you know it's a button — cast is reasonable. JSON: validate then narrow via type guard instead of casting. Library quirks: cast through unknown to flag the unsafety.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `as User` after JSON.parse: any malformed response crashes deep in render. `as any`: defeats the type system entirely.

task: Reject connection in io.use with informative error so the client knows why.
reasoning: new Error(msg) in next() sends the message to client as connect_error event. Attach data field for structured detail.
why_this_is_correct: err.data is sent to client's connect_error handler — clients can show specific UI per code. Generic error.message stays user-facing; structured data for programmatic handling.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Just calling next() on bad token: hangs or accepts. Throw without data: client only gets the message string, can't differentiate causes.

task: Set X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff so browsers don't MIME-sniff responses.
reasoning: Browser MIME-sniffing infers types from content, enabling XSS via mistyped files. nosniff disables sniffing. Set globally.
why_this_is_correct: One header, one line, big impact. Server says 'trust my Content-Type'. Helmet sets this too — explicit version useful when avoiding the full helmet dependency.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Without nosniff: uploaded .txt with HTML content gets rendered as HTML in some browsers — XSS via uploaded files.

task: Lazy initial state for useReducer too — pass an init function as the third arg.
reasoning: Like useState's lazy init. Avoids recomputing expensive initial state on every render.
why_this_is_correct: Third arg is the init function called with the second arg. Runs once on mount even though useReducer is called every render. Date.now() captured once, not on each render.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useReducer(reducer, init(initialItems)): init called every render, recomputes initial state needlessly (only the first call's result is used). Side effects in init re-execute.

task: Express 5 auto-catches async errors. Show before/after.
reasoning: In Express 4, async route handler that throws causes unhandled rejection. Express 5 routes promise rejections to the error middleware automatically.
why_this_is_correct: Express 5: async function as handler is fine — rejection flows to error middleware. Express 4: need asyncHandler wrapper. Knowing which version your app uses prevents head-scratching when an unhandled rejection crashes the process.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Assuming Express 4 needs no wrapping: unhandled rejection warnings escalate to process crash in Node 15+. Wrapping in Express 5: no harm, just redundant.

task: Detect and break circular imports — they cause undefined references and runtime crashes.
reasoning: A imports B; B imports A. At module init time, one side sees the other as undefined. Move shared types/utilities to a third module.
why_this_is_correct: Extract shared dependency to its own module — both features depend on shared, not on each other. Cycle broken. Tooling catches new cycles automatically.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cyclic imports: one side gets undefined at init, calls fail mysteriously. Workarounds (dynamic require, lazy imports) hide the structural issue.

task: Memoize ONLY actually-expensive derivations. Cheap ones benefit nothing from useMemo.
reasoning: useMemo has overhead (dep tracking, allocation). For cheap operations (string concat, simple math), the overhead exceeds the saved cycles.
why_this_is_correct: sort is O(n log n) — worth memoizing. String concat is microseconds — not worth the hook overhead. Profile first; memoize what's actually slow.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping every derivation in useMemo: hook overhead dominates, code harder to read, no measurable perf gain. Eventually devs cargo-cult useMemo everywhere.

task: Server-side handler for client-reported errors with size and rate caps.
reasoning: Client error reports can flood the server if abused. Validate, cap size, optionally per-user rate limit.
why_this_is_correct: Zod validates shape and caps payload sizes. Per-socket rate limit (30/min) prevents log flood. Silent drop above limit avoids amplifying abuse with response noise.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Logging every reported error: malicious client floods logs. No length cap: 100MB stack strings in logs. No userId attribution: errors disconnected from who triggered them.

task: Never log full secrets — log only fingerprints (first 4 + last 4 chars).
reasoning: Accidental secret logging happens. Always log a fingerprint, not the value. Defense in depth even when redaction misses.
why_this_is_correct: Fingerprint lets ops verify which secret loaded without revealing it. Useful when rotating: 'is the new secret deployed?' Pair with the redaction logger from sec-204 for runtime.
what_breaks_naive_approach: console.log(config): JWT_SECRET in logs forever. Skipping fingerprint: ops can't verify which version deployed.

task: Conventions for null vs undefined — TS supports both, picking one consistently helps readability.
reasoning: undefined = 'never set'; null = 'explicitly empty'. Many TS codebases use undefined for both. JSON serializes undefined to missing key but null as null. Be deliberate.
why_this_is_correct: Convention: null for 'cleared' fields that must round-trip to/from DB; optional (undefined) for fields not yet set or omitted. Don't allow both simultaneously — picking one prevents ambiguity in client code.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `bio?: string | null` accepts three states: missing, null, string. Every consumer must check both. Reduces to mostly noise — pick one.

task: In-process async mutex for serializing access to a shared resource.
reasoning: For per-key serialization (state-302) within one process. Simple chain pattern — each lock awaits the previous.
why_this_is_correct: Each lock awaits the previous one's release. work() runs serialized. Per-key mutex allows different keys to run in parallel. Note: pure JS — not cross-process; use Redis lock for that (conc-002).
what_breaks_naive_approach: In-process counter / flag: not async-safe, races between checks. Trying to serialize via Promise.all with shared state: defeats the purpose.
